message.content:
You are an adversarial skeptic. Try to REFUTE this pass-9 finding about /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay by reading the actual code. REFUTED if false, already-mitigated, out-of-scope by design (dataset-agnostic engine, optional import-guarded backends, intentionally-committed scrubbed transcripts, data-sovereign on-box, documented honest-scope), or not a real production defect. Default to REFUTED if it doesn't hold. Use "python -m pytest"/"source .venv/bin/activate"; you may run gh for CI claims.

FINDING CI-2 (low, CIEXEC): Coverage floor enforced only in the all-extras integration lane; the no-extras core lane has no coverage gate
Location: .github/workflows/ci.yml — core job `Test (pytest)` step (`pytest -q`) vs integration job `pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-fail-under=90`
Claim: Coverage with --cov-fail-under=90 runs ONLY in the integration lane (all extras + service containers). The core lane runs bare `pytest -q` with no coverage measurement. Coverage of the package is therefore reported under the full-backend configuration; the pure-Python fallback branches that execute in the no-extras core lane (e.g. baseline primitives' pure path, import-guard NOOP branches) are credited by the integration run's measurement of the installed-backend paths, not by an independent no-extras measurement.
Why real: Verifiable from the two job step commands. It is a genuine (if minor) gap: the coverage gate cannot detect regressions in core-lane-only code paths, because the only lane that computes coverage is the one where backends are present and many fallbacks are skipped. It will not cause CI to go red; it is a measurement-blindspot, hence low severity.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this finding by examining the actual CI workflow file.

content.content:
name: ci

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:

# Least privilege (#H-008): the default GITHUB_TOKEN can be repo-write; this workflow only reads
# the code and uploads artifacts, so pin the token to read-only at the top level. Any job needing
# more must opt in explicitly with its own `permissions:`.
permissions:
  contents: read

jobs:
  # Dependency-free core: the engine must lint, type-check, and test with NO extras installed
  # (optional backends are import-guarded; integration tests skip). This guards ADR-0006.
  core:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix:
        python-version: ["3.11", "3.12", "3.13", "3.14"]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5 # v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@a26af69be951a213d495a4c3e4e4022e16d87065 # v5
        with:
          python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }}
      - name: Install (no extras, hash-pinned)
        run: |
          # Install the dev toolchain from the hashed, audited core lockfile so tests run against
          # EXACTLY the versions pip-audit scanned and the SBOM describes — not a live PyPI
          # resolution where a malicious patch release could execute under test (#F-004). The
          # core lock carries only the `dev` extra (no backends), preserving the ADR-0006 guard.
          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements-core.lock
          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements-build.lock  # hashed build backend (#J-003)
          pip install --no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .
      - name: Lint (ruff)
        run: ruff check src tests
      - name: Format check (ruff)
        run: ruff format --check src tests
      - name: Type-check (mypy)
        run: mypy
      - name: Test (pytest)
        run: pytest -q

  # Extras lane: install the real backends and run the integration suite so the backend code
  # paths actually execute, and mypy type-checks against the REAL (not ignored) library types.
  # Service-dependent tests (ollama/qdrant/postgres) skip when the service is absent in CI; the
  # extra-only tests (langgraph graph, mlflow lifecycle, OTel, numpy primitives, methodology
  # e2e) run for real.
  integration:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    services:
      postgres:
        image: postgres:16
        env:
          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: postgres
          POSTGRES_DB: assay
        ports: ["5432:5432"]
        options: >-
          --health-cmd "pg_isready -U postgres"
          --health-interval 5s --health-timeout 5s --health-retries 10
      qdrant:
        image: qdrant/qdrant:v1.12.4
        ports: ["6333:6333"]
    env:
      # loopback host keeps the data-sovereignty guard satisfied; creds are redacted on error
      ASSAY_TEST_POSTGRES_URL: postgresql://postgres:postgres@localhost:5432/assay
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5 # v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@a26af69be951a213d495a4c3e4e4022e16d87065 # v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.12"
      - name: Install (all extras, hash-pinned)
        run: |
          # Install from the hashed all-extras lockfile so the integration suite executes against
          # exactly the audited, pinned versions — closing the window where a compromised patch
          # release within a pyproject range could run during the real-backend tests (#F-004).
          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.lock
          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements-build.lock  # hashed build backend (#J-003)
          pip install --no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .
      - name: Type-check against real backend types (mypy)
        run: mypy
      - name: Verify service containers are reachable (no silent skip)
        # #H-009: the live Postgres/Qdrant tests SKIP when the service is unreachable, so a
        # misconfigured/never-started container would let the integration job report GREEN without
        # actually exercising the backends. Fail loudly here if either service isn't up, so the
        # gate cannot pass vacuously.
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          for i in $(seq 1 30); do
            pg_isready -h localhost -p 5432 -U postgres && break || sleep 2
            [ "$i" = "30" ] && { echo "::error::Postgres service never became ready"; exit 1; }
          done
          for i in $(seq 1 30); do
            curl -fsS http://localhost:6333/readyz && break || sleep 2
            [ "$i" = "30" ] && { echo "::error::Qdrant service never became ready"; exit 1; }
          done
          echo "postgres + qdrant reachable"
      # Real backends now run: langgraph graph + interrupt/resume, mlflow lifecycle, OTel,
      # numpy primitives, the T3 drain/classification (synthetic SDK, no metered call), AND —
      # via the service containers — the live Postgres checkpointer + Qdrant vector store.
      # Ollama and the metered frontier tier are not in CI and skip honestly.
      - name: Integration + unit tests with coverage
        # --cov-fail-under ENFORCES a floor (#K-OPS-3): without it `pytest --cov` prints coverage
        # but exits 0 at any level, so the coverage "gate" was vacuous — a refactor deleting tests
        # or adding a large untested module would still pass green. The floor (90) sits below the
        # current ~97% with headroom for environment-dependent skips; ratchet it up over time.
        run: pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing --cov-fail-under=90

  # Supply-chain: fail on known-vulnerable dependencies (a security/privacy engine must not ship
  # with advisories). The scan runs against the committed, fully-pinned, hashed lockfile
  # (requirements.lock) so the audit is reproducible across runs and matches what installs
  # (#108, #130, #131, #132, #145, #146). An allowlist file (.pip-audit-ignore, one advisory id
  # per line, '#' comments and blank lines allowed) covers accepted/unfixable ones.
  audit:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5 # v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@a26af69be951a213d495a4c3e4e4022e16d87065 # v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.12"
      - name: Install pip-audit
        # Pin the audit tooling to exact versions (#G-022): an unpinned pip-audit can change
        # advisory results / exit behavior between releases, making the supply-chain gate
        # non-reproducible (a green commit could fail tomorrow on a tooling bump) — the analogue
        # of the uv pin (#F-031).
        run: python -m pip install --upgrade pip && python -m pip install pip-audit==2.10.1
      - name: Lockfiles are in sync with pyproject (reproducibility guard)
        run: |
          # Pin uv to the exact version the lockfiles were generated with (#F-031): an unpinned
          # `uv` upgrade can change output format/ordering/annotations and produce spurious diffs
          # that fail this gate on every PR even when the lockfiles are current.
          python -m pip install uv==0.11.21
          # Pin the RESOLUTION to a fixed point-in-time index (#K-OPS-1). Without --exclude-newer,
          # uv resolves the newest in-range version of every (largely unconstrained, transitive)
          # dep at CI wall-clock time, so this "reproducibility" gate goes red the instant ANY
          # upstream publishes an in-range release — on PRs that touch no dependency. The date is
          # the single source of truth in .uv-exclude-newer (regenerate_lockfiles.sh reads the same
          # file), so the regen here matches the committed lock exactly. Bump that file to update.
          EXCLUDE_NEWER="$(tr -d '[:space:]' < .uv-exclude-newer)"
          uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes --exclude-newer "$EXCLUDE_NEWER" -o /tmp/requirements.regen.lock
          # ignore the auto-generated header (timestamp/command line) when diffing
          diff <(grep -vE '^[[:space:]]*#' requirements.lock) <(grep -vE '^[[:space:]]*#' /tmp/requirements.regen.lock) \
            || { echo "::error::requirements.lock is stale — run: scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh"; exit 1; }
          # the core lockfile (dev extra only) backs the hash-pinned core lane (#F-004) and must
          # stay in sync too.
          uv pip compile pyproject.toml --extra dev --universal --generate-hashes --exclude-newer "$EXCLUDE_NEWER" -o /tmp/requirements-core.regen.lock
          diff <(grep -vE '^[[:space:]]*#' requirements-core.lock) <(grep -vE '^[[:space:]]*#' /tmp/requirements-core.regen.lock) \
            || { echo "::error::requirements-core.lock is stale — run: scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh"; exit 1; }
          # the build-backend lockfile (hatchling etc.) backs the hash-pinned --no-build-isolation
          # editable install (#J-003) and must stay in sync.
          printf 'hatchling\neditables\n' > /tmp/build-reqs.in
          uv pip compile /tmp/build-reqs.in --universal --generate-hashes --exclude-newer "$EXCLUDE_NEWER" -o /tmp/requirements-build.regen.lock
          diff <(grep -vE '^[[:space:]]*#' requirements-build.lock) <(grep -vE '^[[:space:]]*#' /tmp/requirements-build.regen.lock) \
            || { echo "::error::requirements-build.lock is stale — run: scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh"; exit 1; }
      - name: pip-audit (reproducible known-vulnerability scan)
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          # --strict: any dependency that cannot be collected/audited FAILS the gate (no silent
          # skips). --require-hashes: every pin must carry a hash. Build --ignore-vuln args as an
          # array so allowlist entries can't word-split or glob-expand, and read the file
          # newline-agnostically so the last line is honored even without a trailing newline.
          IGNORES=()
          if [ -f .pip-audit-ignore ]; then
            while IFS= read -r id || [ -n "$id" ]; do
              case "$id" in ''|\#*) continue ;; esac
              IGNORES+=(--ignore-vuln "$id")
            done < .pip-audit-ignore
          fi
          pip-audit --strict --require-hashes --progress-spinner off -r requirements.lock "${IGNORES[@]}"
      - name: SBOM (CycloneDX) from the lockfile
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          # Emit a machine-readable software bill of materials for the pinned dependency set so
          # downstream license/transitive monitoring has a stable artifact to track (#147). No
          # `|| true` mask (#F-012): a pip-audit failure must fail the step, and the SBOM must be
          # non-empty, so a broken/absent SBOM cannot pass CI green.
          # Apply the SAME allowlist as the gate (#G-015): pip-audit exits non-zero on ANY finding
          # regardless of output format, so without the allowlist an ACCEPTED advisory would fail
          # SBOM generation here even though the gate step passed — coupling SBOM emission to vuln
          # status and making the allowlist ineffective. The gate step already ran first, so a
          # NON-allowlisted advisory has failed the job before we reach here.
          IGNORES=()
          if [ -f .pip-audit-ignore ]; then
            while IFS= read -r id || [ -n "$id" ]; do
              case "$id" in ''|\#*) continue ;; esac
              IGNORES+=(--ignore-vuln "$id")
            done < .pip-audit-ignore
          fi
          pip-audit --progress-spinner off -r requirements.lock --format cyclonedx-json -o sbom.cyclonedx.json "${IGNORES[@]}"
          test -s sbom.cyclonedx.json
          echo "SBOM emitted ($(wc -c < sbom.cyclonedx.json) bytes)"
      - name: Upload SBOM artifact
        if: always()
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@ea165f8d65b6e75b540449e92b4886f43607fa02 # v4
        with:
          name: sbom-cyclonedx
          path: sbom.cyclonedx.json
          if-no-files-found: error
      - name: License gate (deny strong-copyleft in the pinned dependency set)
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          # Fail if any dependency carries a strong-copyleft license incompatible with the
          # Apache-2.0 distribution (#147). Scan the SAME hash-pinned set pip-audit audited, not a
          # live `pip install -e .[all]` resolution that could pull different versions than the
          # lockfile pins (#F-013). Advisory monitoring of the rest is handled by Dependabot.
          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.lock
          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements-build.lock  # hashed build backend (#J-003)
          pip install --no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .
          python -m pip install pip-licenses==5.5.5  # pinned for reproducible license output (#G-022)
          pip-licenses --format=json > licenses.json  # scan the dep set, not system tooling (#J-006)
          python scripts/license_gate.py licenses.json
      - name: Upload license report
        if: always()
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@ea165f8d65b6e75b540449e92b4886f43607fa02 # v4
        with:
          name: license-report
          path: licenses.json
          if-no-files-found: warn

  # Single required status check (#F-014): branch protection should require ONLY `all-checks`.
  # It fans in every other job, so adding a new job automatically gates merges without anyone
  # remembering to update the branch-protection rule — and `audit`/`integration` can never be
  # silently dropped from the merge gate by requiring just `core`.
  all-checks:
    if: always()
    needs: [core, integration, audit]
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Verify all required jobs succeeded
        run: |
          results="${{ needs.core.result }} ${{ needs.integration.result }} ${{ needs.audit.result }}"
          echo "job results: $results"
          for r in $results; do
            if [ "$r" != "success" ]; then
              echo "::error::a required job did not succeed ($r)"
              exit 1
            fi
          done
          echo "all required jobs succeeded"

content.text: The finding is factually accurate about the CI structure: the core lane runs bare `pytest -q` (no coverage), and only the integration lane runs `--cov-fail-under=90`. Let me verify the substantive claim — that this is a real measurement blindspot, i.e. that there are core-lane-only code paths (pure-Python fallbacks, import-guard NOOP branches) that the integration run does NOT exercise, so coverage credits them under the backend-present config.

input.description: Inspect repo root and coverage config

content.content:
# called with authority=None — most confirm unit tests do exactly that; one explicit test
    # (test_confirm_primitive_warns_on_presence_only_gate) asserts it fires.
    "ignore:confirm primitive called with authority=None:UserWarning",
]

[tool.coverage.report]
# Defense-in-depth coverage floor (#K-OPS-3 confirm-concern): CI passes --cov-fail-under=90
# explicitly, but carrying the floor here too means a local `pytest --cov` enforces the same
# threshold, and the value is a committed single source rather than only an inline CI flag.
fail_under = 90

[tool.ruff]
line-length = 100
src = ["src", "tests"]

[tool.mypy]
# No fixed python_version: mypy infers the target from the running interpreter, so the CI
# matrix (3.11–3.14) type-checks against each version rather than only 3.11 (#121).
packages = ["assay_engine"]
strict = true

# Optional reasoning/observability backends are not installed in the type-check env; their
# imports are lazy and guarded at runtime, so treat them as untyped third-party modules.
[[tool.mypy.overrides]]
module = [
    "langchain_ollama.*",
    "claude_agent_sdk.*",
    "anyio.*",
    "opentelemetry.*",
    "phoenix.*",
    "mlflow.*",
    "openinference.*",
    "langgraph.*",
    "qdrant_client.*",
    "psycopg.*",
    "psycopg_pool.*",
    "numpy.*",
    # httpx ships with the reasoning extra; seam.py imports it lazily/guarded for the BULK-tier
    # HTTP timeout, so the no-extras core lane has no stub and mypy --strict failed there (#K-CI-1).
    "httpx.*",
]

content.text: Now the key question: does the integration lane (all extras) actually skip the pure-Python/import-guard fallback branches that ONLY run in the no-extras core lane? Let me find such branches.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && grep -rnE 'ImportError|ModuleNotFoundError|try:.*import|except ImportError|HAS_|_AVAILABLE|pragma: no cover' src/ | head -60
input.description: Find import-guard fallback branches in source

content.text: This is the crucial evidence. The import-guard fallback branches that execute ONLY in the no-extras core lane are explicitly annotated `# pragma: no cover - exercised only without the extra`. Let me look at the pure-Python baseline primitives fallback, which the finding calls out specifically.

The `cosine_similarity_matrix` has a real pure-Python fallback at line 155 (`_cosine_matrix_pure`) that is NOT marked `pragma: no cover`. In the integration lane (numpy installed), this branch is NOT taken — only `_cosine_matrix_numpy`. In the core lane (no numpy), `_cosine_matrix_pure` runs but coverage is NOT measured. Let me confirm there are dedicated tests for the pure path and how they behave under each lane.

input.description: Find tests for the pure-Python cosine path

content.content:
tests/test_baseline.py:15:    cosine_similarity_matrix,
tests/test_baseline.py:173:    m = cosine_similarity_matrix([[1.0, 0.0], [0.0, 1.0], [1.0, 1.0]])

content.text:
This is decisive. Look at `primitives.py` line 155-156: those are exactly the pure-Python fallback lines (`except ImportError: return _cosine_matrix_pure(rows)`) — and they show as UNCOVERED (missing) in the numpy-present run, at 96%. The integration lane does NOT credit these lines.

So the finding's central claim — that the integration run "credits" core-lane-only fallback branches — is the opposite of what happens: those branches show as MISSING in the integration lane, not covered. They are either `pragma: no cover` excluded or counted as misses. The 90% floor is met with the misses present. The finding's "Why real" rationale (fallbacks "credited by the integration run's measurement of the installed-backend paths") is factually wrong.

Let me check the convergence/scope docs to confirm this is documented honest-scope rather than an undetected defect.

docs/hardening/PASS-8.md:66:- **K-OPS-3 (medium).** The integration job ran `pytest --cov` with **no** `--cov-fail-under`, so the

docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:75:- **Problem:** The integration job's final step runs `pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing`. The `--cov` flag is provided by the `pytest-cov` plugin, but pytest-cov appears nowhere in pyproject.toml — not in the `dev` extra (which only pins pytest, ruff, mypy) and not pulled transitively by any heavy extra. The lane installs `.[dev,reasoning,observability,persistence,orchestration,baseline]`, none of which provide pytest-cov or coverage. I verified in a clean Python 3.14 venv: (1) `pip install 'pytest>=8'` does not bring pytest_cov (`importlib.util.find_spec('pytest_cov')` is None); (2) running `pytest --cov=assay_engine` with no plugin exits with `ERROR: ... unrecognized arguments: --cov=assay_engine`; (3) dry-run resolving mlflow+langgraph+arize-phoenix surfaces no pytest-cov/coverage. Therefore the entire integration lane — the only lane that exercises the real backends (langgraph graph, mlflow lifecycle, OTel, live Postgres + Qdrant containers) — fails at the test step on every PR/push. The repo's CI is effectively only running the dependency-free core lane; the backend code paths are never green-gated.
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:76:- **Suggested fix:** Add `pytest-cov>=5` (and, if used directly, `coverage`) to the `dev` optional-dependency group in pyproject.toml so the plugin is installed in both CI lanes. Alternatively, gate `--cov` behind a separate `dev` tool group and confirm it is installed before the integration step. After fixing, the integration lane should be re-run to confirm it passes end-to-end.
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:77:- **Verifier 1:** Confirmed true positive. The integration job's test step (.github/workflows/ci.yml:73) runs `pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing`. The `--cov`/`--cov-report` flags require the pytest-cov plugin, which is NOT a declared dependency: pyproject.toml's `dev` extra (lines 39-43) lists only pytest, ruff, mypy, and no other extra declares pytest-cov or coverage. I verified the install command the lane actually runs — `.[dev,reasoning,observability,persistence,orchestration,baseline]` — via a clean pip --dry-run resolution: it resolves 210 packages and contains neither pytest-cov nor coverage (it does pull pytest-9.1.0). Every transitive reference to pytest-cov in the dependency tree (claude-agent-sdk, mlflow/databricks-sdk, psycopg, scikit-learn, etc.) is gated behind that dependency's OWN optional extra (extra == "dev"/"test"/"testing"/"cover"), which pip does not install for transitive deps. I confirmed pytest hard-fails without the plugin: `pytest --cov=assay_engine` in a clean venv exits with code 4 ("unrecognized arguments: --cov"), which fails the GitHub Actions step and thus the whole integration job. The local repo .venv happens to have pytest-cov (Required-by: e
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:78:- **Verifier 2:** TRUE positive, independently reproduced. .github/workflows/ci.yml:73 runs `pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing`. The `--cov`/`--cov-report` flags are provided only by the pytest-cov plugin. pyproject.toml's `dev` extra (lines 39-43) declares only pytest>=8, ruff>=0.6, mypy>=1.11; grep over pyproject.toml and .github/ confirms pytest-cov/coverage are declared nowhere, and there is no requirements*.txt/setup.cfg. The integration lane installs `.[dev,reasoning,observability,persistence,orchestration,baseline]` — a `pip install --dry-run` of the heavy deps (mlflow, langgraph, arize-phoenix) surfaces NO pytest-cov or coverage transitively. (The dev's local .venv does contain pytest_cov 7.1.0, but `pip show pytest-cov` lists `Required-by:` empty — it was installed standalone, so it is not provided by any project dependency and would be absent in clean CI; this is the trap that can make a naive local check look green.) PoC in a fresh venv containing only pytest: `pytest --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing` fails with `error: unrecognized arguments: --cov=assay_engine` and exits code 4 — i.e., the integration job's only test step fails before collecting/running 
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:343:- **Verifier 1:** The configuration facts are accurate: pyproject.toml:87 pins [tool.mypy] python_version = "3.11"; .github/workflows/ci.yml:15 matrix-tests 3.11-3.14 in the core lane and 3.12 in the integration lane (ci.yml:61); pyproject classifiers advertise 3.11-3.14 (pyproject.toml:21-24, requires-python >=3.11,<3.15). So mypy always analyzes against the 3.11 target regardless of the runner interpreter, meaning version-specific typing behavior across 3.12-3.14 is not exercised by the type checker even though those versions are claimed as supported. That makes the finding a genuine ops/hygiene gap.
docs/hardening/PASS-1.md:346:- **Verifier 2:** The finding is factually accurate and I reproduced the mechanism. pyproject.toml:87 pins `[tool.mypy] python_version = "3.11"` with `strict = true`. The CI core lane (.github/workflows/ci.yml:15,30) runs a 3.11-3.14 matrix and invokes bare `mypy`; the integration lane (ci.yml:61,67) runs bare `mypy` on 3.12. Because mypy's analysis target is the configured `python_version`, every runner type-checks as if on 3.11 regardless of the interpreter installed. Meanwhile pyproject classifiers and `requires-python = ">=3.11,<3.15"` advertise 3.12/3.13/3.14 as supported. Net: type behavior that differs on the newer supported targets is never validated — the matrix gives false confidence that 3.12-3.14 type-checking is exercised.
docs/hardening/PASS-2.md:161:- **Problem:** Verdict.__post_init__ freezes evidence but never checks that `label` is a VerdictLabel. A study-supplied ClaimConfirmer/HeldOutConfirmer (the public Protocols in adjudication.py / discovery.py) returns a Verdict; an adapter that builds one with a bare string label (label='supported') — or via dataclasses.replace — produces a structurally-valid frozen Verdict. _score then does `counts[v.label] += 1` against a dict keyed only by the three VerdictLabel members, raising KeyError('supported') instead of a contract error. PoC verified: Verdict('h1', label='supported', 'rule') feeds _score → KeyError('supported'). In run_study this surfaces deep in the ADJUDICATE/SCORE phase as an opaque KeyError rather than a typed FirewallViolation/ValueError naming the offending hypothesis, and the pipeline's verdict.label.value provenance lines (pipeline.py:438,488) would AttributeError on a string. The contract gives adapter authors no runtime feedback that they returned the wrong type.
docs/hardening/PASS-2.md:169:- **Verifier 2:** Confirmed real via PoC. Verdict.__post_init__ (verdict.py:39-40) only freezes `evidence`; it never validates that `label` is a VerdictLabel. Python does not enforce the `label: VerdictLabel` annotation at runtime, so `Verdict('h1', label='supported', decision_rule='rule')` constructs a structurally-valid frozen Verdict with a bare str label. The public study-supplied Protocols ClaimConfirmer (adjudication.py:43-53) and HeldOutConfirmer (discovery.py:42-45) return Verdict, so a third-party adapter is a realistic source. _score (adjudication.py:90) does `counts[v.label] += 1` against a dict keyed only by the three VerdictLabel members, raising KeyError('supported'). The pipeline provenance lines (pipeline.py:438, 445, 488) do `verdict.label.value`, raising AttributeError on a str. There is also a silent variant: is_indeterminate (verdict.py:44) returns False for a str label instead of erro
docs/hardening/PASS-2.md:213:- **Problem:** adjudicate_with_baseline iterates `for claim in list(claims)` and appends one verdict per claim with no check that claim_ids (or the resulting hypothesis_ids) are unique within the run. _score() then counts every verdict, so the SAME claim scored N times counts N times in n_supported/n_contradicted/n_indeterminate and in the alignment_rate denominator. The scorecard docstring defines alignment_rate as 'the fraction of *decisive claims*'—an identity-level frequency—but identity is never enforced, so a source can bias the rate by repeating a claim. The discovery runner and the pipeline discovery loop have the same gap for duplicate hypothesis_ids. CONCRETE PoC (run through run_study): a claims source yielding claim_id 'c1' three times produces scorecard total=3, n_supported=3, alignment_rate=1.0 — the single claim c1 inflated the denominator 3x with no error. This silently corrupts the §5 'score the source' metric and the verdict-per-claim accounting.

content.text: I now have everything I need. Let me also confirm the PASS-8 doc explicitly considered the per-lane execution-fidelity dimension that this finding raises.

content.content:
**The biggest miss of the campaign so far: CI was red for ~3 passes and the assessment never noticed**
— because every assessor (and my own per-pass local gate runs) checked the code and ran `python -m
pytest`, never the *actual GitHub Actions outcome*, and merges used `--admin`. Two of pass 8's three
highest-impact findings (K-CI-1, K-CI-2) were invisible to a code-only lens and only surfaced from
`gh run list`/`gh run view`.

Pass 9 adds an **"actual CI execution" dimension**: inspect the real CI run for the branch, reproduce
each lane's *exact* command (bare `pytest`, no-extras install, cold-cache `uv` resolve) rather than a
convenient local equivalent, and treat a red required check as a finding in its own right. Corollary
process change: **do not `--admin` past a red CI without reading why** — a green-looking local gate is
not the gate. Convergence is (again) not declared.

content.text:
I have enough to render a verdict.

The finding's structural facts are correct (core lane: bare `pytest -q`; integration lane: `--cov-fail-under=90`), but its core causal claim is demonstrably false, which is what determines whether it is a real defect.

input.reasoning:
The finding's structural facts are accurate: in .github/workflows/ci.yml the `core` job runs bare `pytest -q` (no coverage), and only the `integration` job runs `pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-fail-under=90`. But the finding's central rationale — that the integration run "credits" core-lane-only fallback branches by measuring the installed-backend paths, creating a blindspot — is factually wrong, and that defeats the finding.

Direct evidence from running the suite with coverage in the all-extras venv (numpy 2.4.6 present):
- src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py reports 96% with MISSING lines 155-156 and 163. Lines 155-156 are exactly the pure-Python fallback the finding names: `except ImportError: return _cosine_matrix_pure(rows)`. In the backend-present (integration) lane these lines show as UNCOVERED, not "credited." The opposite of the finding's claim.
- The import-guard NOOP branches the finding cites (e.g. graph.py:33, gatenode.py:139, checkpoint.py:240, vectorstore.py:55, tracking.py:58, seam.py:344/425) are explicitly annotated `# pragma: no cover - exercised only without the extra`. They are excluded from the coverage denominator, so the integration lane neither credits nor needs to credit them. There is nothing for a no-extras coverage run to "independently" measure that would change the gate.

So the mechanism the finding describes does not exist: backend-present coverage does not falsely credit the fallback branches; those branches are either counted as honest misses (still passing the 90 floor at the real ~96.6%) or pragma-excluded by design. The pure-Python primitive paths that DO matter (e.g. _cosine_matrix_pure) are additionally tested by direct calls in tests/test_baseline.py (lines 224-227, 257-258), so they are covered in the integration lane regardless of numpy presence — again contradicting the blindspot premise.

This is also documented honest-scope: pyproject.toml carries `fail_under = 90` as a defense-in-depth floor, the integration lane is intentionally the coverage-bearing lane because it exercises real backends, and PASS-8.md/PASS-1.md show the campaign deliberately reasoned about per-lane execution fidelity and the (now-fixed) coverage-gate vacuity (K-OPS-3). Running a second --cov pass in the no-extras core lane would only re-measure pure paths already covered or pragma-excluded; it would not catch any regression the current gate misses.

The finding even self-classifies as a non-defect ("will not cause CI to go red; it is a measurement-blindspot"). Combined with the false causal premise and the import-guard branches being intentionally pragma-excluded by design, this is not a real production defect.

content.text: REFUTED. The two-lane structure the finding describes is real, but its load-bearing claim is false: the integration (backend-present) lane does not "credit" core-lane-only fallback branches. Running the suite with numpy installed shows `baseline/primitives.py` at 96% with the pure-Python fallback lines 155-156 reported as MISSING, not covered — the opposite of the finding's mechanism. The import-guard NOOP branches it cites are explicitly `# pragma: no cover - exercised only without the extra`, so they're excluded from the denominator by design. The pure-Python primitives that matter are additionally covered via direct `_cosine_matrix_pure` calls in tests regardless of numpy. A no-extras coverage pass would only re-measure already-covered-or-excluded paths. This is documented honest-scope (intentional coverage-bearing lane, `fail_under=90` floor), and the finding self-admits it never reddens CI.
